trajectory_summary: The call opens with an unusually well-qualified, self-disclosing customer and ends with an unstructured callback promise to a lead the rep was not prepared to work. Energy was present in the customer from the start — Liz volunteered detailed technical information, a specific need, and bill figures without prompting. The rep failed to match that energy with preparation: the utility company was not pre-checked, the orphaned-flow checklist was not completed, and the exit was structurally fragile — a vague 'callback later' with no locked time. The call's shape is energy_leaked: the customer brought the energy; the rep let it drain out.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'no_appointment_callback_promised' — not a booked sit, not a DQ, but a soft callback with no locked time. Per schema analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, a callback without a locked time has materially higher no-show risk than a scheduled appointment. The lead is not dead but is structurally fragile.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Pre-call utility eligibility check — preparation discipline
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Apparently, we're not able to work with WASCO, because as of right now, their the utility company is not accepting any solar applications and does not have any plans to reopen their solar program at the at the moment.
primary_coaching_focus.why: This is a well-engaged lead with a specific need (add-on panels + battery backup) who ended the call without a booking because the rep discovered a checkable eligibility condition mid-call. WASCO eligibility was accessible in the rep's tools during the call (the rep's own words: 'this looks like it was updated as of a year ago'), which means it was accessible before dialing. The manager should use this call to establish a pre-call prep habit: non-standard utility co-ops get an eligibility check before the dial, not during bill collection.

key_moments.quote: Just the I mean, my winter power bills are, all the way up into the 5 hundreds.
key_moments.stewart_read: Liz hands the rep a concrete bill-pain anchor in the first 45 seconds — winter bills in the $500s. The rep responds with 'Oh, jeez.' and then immediately moves to address confirmation. The bill is captured but never flipped. Per the schema, capture alone is necessary but not sufficient — the move is to flip the bill into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The rep had the number and walked past the leverage.

key_moments.quote: Oh, what I'm surprised you didn't get this information. I've provided all of this information.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a process-competence failure signal, not a legitimacy-anxiety moment. Liz expected her prior form data to be on file. The rep's response ('Sometimes, when it gets pulled up, it's not always the right one. I don't know why. It just swaps it with the utility') is a vague deflection that neither resolves the concern nor acknowledges the gap. The correct move is honest specificity: acknowledge the data gap, take ownership briefly, and move forward without dwelling. The deflection leaves the process failure unaddressed and slightly erodes the credibility the intro established.

Liz volunteers a specific product request — battery backup — unprompted. This is a high-value enthusiasm signal: she knows what she wants, she has an existing system, and she is describing a specific gap (undersized system, one broken panel, no storage). The rep acknowledges it ('K. Battery backup. Let me get that down.') and confirms the add-on scope correctly. The procedural handling is adequate. The coaching gap is tonal: the rep treats this as a checkbox item rather than a moment of genuine alignment. A warmer acknowledgment — 'Battery backup is exactly what the Powerwall is built for, and it pairs well with an add-on' — would have elevated the customer's confidence that she called the right company. The rep's flat acknowledgment is a missed warmth moment, not a substantive product failure.

key_moments.quote: I've this property was previously owned by a guy that owns an electric company. And so Oh, okay. I'm assuming that things just fell off the truck and landed on his house.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep enters orphaned territory (existing solar on property) but does not complete the schema-defined orphaned_flow checklist. Specifically: (1) own-vs-lease was never asked — the rep confirmed it wasn't Pink Solar but did not establish ownership status of the existing system; (2) system operational status was never explicitly confirmed — Liz mentioned one broken panel but the rep never asked 'Is the system currently working?' as the checklist requires. The rep's Pink Solar check is correct procedure, but it is the only orphaned_flow item completed. The ownership and operational-status gaps are schema-defined hard-DQ triggers if the answers are wrong.
